scaffold:
Step 1: From “10 machines make 10 widgets in 10 minutes,” the group rate is 10 widgets per 10 minutes.

Step 2: That means each machine makes 1 widget in 10 minutes.

Step 3: So 200 machines working at the same rate will make 200 widgets in 10 minutes.

FINAL: 10 minutes
wrong_scaffold:
Step 1: Start by finding the production rate per machine.  
If 10 machines make 10 widgets in 10 minutes, then together they make 10 widgets in 10 minutes.

Step 2: Divide the total widgets by the number of machines to get the output per machine in 10 minutes.  
So each machine makes \(10 \div 10 = 1\) widget every 10 minutes.

Step 3: Scale up to 200 machines.  
With 200 machines, in 10 minutes they would make \(200 \times 1 = 200\) widgets.

Step 4: Since the number of widgets increased from 10 to 200, that is \(200 \div 10 = 20\) times as many widgets.

Step 5: Multiply the original time by this factor.  
So the time needed is \(10 \times 20 = 200\) minutes.

FINAL: 200
compressed:
10 machines make 10 widgets in 10 min => 1 machine makes 1 widget in 10 min (1:1 machine/widget ratio, working in parallel).

For 200 machines making 200 widgets, the same rate applies: each machine makes 1 widget in 10 min, so 200 machines make 200 widgets simultaneously in 10 min.

Check: rate stays constant; no extra time is needed because output scales with machines.

FINAL: 10
